retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: The 2016 Antenatal Late Preterm Steroids (ALPS) trial was a double-blind, placebo-controlled, randomized controlled trial that studied the effect of antenatal betamethasone in 2831 patients with a singleton pregnancy between 34 and 36 6/7 weeks EGA at high risk of PTB within the next 7 days and before 37 weeks EGA who had not previously received steroids. [34] This study's results showed that antenatal betamethasone significantly reduced the risk of neonatal respiratory morbidity in the study population. However, a secondary analysis of this trial found that those with sPTL or PPROM had lower rates of neonatal respiratory complications when compared to those with indicated PTB, irrespective of betamethasone exposure in the late preterm period. [35] Antenatal corticosteroids also significantly increase the risk of neonatal hypoglycemia. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: When given, 1 of 2 antenatal corticosteroid regiments should be used as follows: Two doses of betamethasone 12 mg intramuscularly (IM), given 24 hours apart Four doses of dexamethasone 6 mg IM, given every 12 hours [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: ACOG and WHO recommend considering a single repeat course of antenatal corticosteroids (ie, "rescue steroids") in individuals who have received 1 previous course of steroids at least 7 to 14 days prior and are currently. A rescue course may consist of either a standard 48-hour regimen described above or a single dose of betamethasone, which evidence suggests is also effective after initial standard therapy. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: ACOG supports SMFM's recommendations; they also recommend against administering steroids to patients with intraamniotic infection. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: Antenatal corticosteroids improve neonatal outcomes when given to individuals at high risk of PTB; these are most effective between 48 hours and 7 days before PTB. [2] For these reasons, antenatal corticosteroids are universally recommended for patients at risk of PTB between viability and 34 weeks EGA. ACOG, the Society for Maternal-Fetal Medicine (SMFM), and the WAPM-PMF guidelines recommend considering antenatal corticosteroids in the periviable period, down to 22 0/7 weeks if neonatal resuscitation is planned. [2] However, beyond 34 weeks, recommendations vary across professional organizations.

retrieved_docs.title: HELLP Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should be referred immediately to a gynecologist to consider prompt delivery as it is the only effective treatment. Betamethasone administration is recommended for fetal lung maturity when the patient presents at <34 weeks of gestation. Magnesium sulfate should be initiated at the time of admission to prevent maternal seizures and neuroprotective effects on the fetus/neonate. In two large double-blind, randomized trials, there was no proven benefit to administering dexamethasone in patients with HELLP syndrome as it did not improve maternal or perinatal/infant outcomes. [18] [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Budesonide -- Indications -- Croup
retrieved_docs.content: In very low birth weight infants, intratracheal administration of budesonide-surfactant combination results in reduced incidence of bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) or composite outcome of BPD or death. [17] Besides, early initiation of treatment with 1 mg of budesonide inhalation suspension appears to reduce overall corticosteroid usage and the need for mechanical ventilation in premature, very low birth weight babies at risk for chronic pulmonary disease. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guideline [NG25] recommends considering antenatal corticosteroids between 34 and 35 6/7 weeks EGA.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The beneficial effect of antenatal corticosteroid use after 34 weeks of gestation is controversial due to the lack of information in long term developmental outcomes. [44] Maternal antenatal corticosteroids are recommended for possible preterm delivery in the next seven days between 23rd and 34th week gestational age. [45] Some institutions offer antenatal corticosteroids at 22 weeks if anticipating delivery within the next week. Despite multiple interventions targeting various etiologies, the goal of preventing preterm birth remains elusive.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome, or RDS, is a common cause of respiratory distress in a newborn, presenting within hours after birth, most often immediately after delivery. RDS primarily affects preterm neonates, and infrequently, term infants. The incidence of RDS is inversely proportional to the gestational age of the infant, with more severe disease in the smaller and more premature neonates. While treatment modalities, including antenatal corticosteroids, surfactants, and advanced respiratory care of the neonate, have improved the outcomes for patients affected by RDS, it continues to be a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in the preterm infant.

retrieved_docs.title: Topical Corticosteroids -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy considerations: A systemic review suggests that maternal use of topical corticosteroids, regardless of potency, is not associated with significant adverse pregnancy outcomes. However, there could be a potential link between cumulative doses of potent corticosteroids and low birth weight; additional research is required. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: The Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG) Green-Top Guideline Number 74 recommends offering antenatal corticosteroids up until 34 6/7 weeks EGA. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Preterm Labor -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: WAPM-PMF recommends individualizing treatment and suggests a 34 0/7 weeks EGA cutoff for treatment with antenatal corticosteroids. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal MIS-C -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment for neonatal MIS-C is largely extrapolated from the evidence from the treatment of adults and older children. [21] Intravenous immunoglobulin and corticosteroids are used to manage MIS-C to improve symptoms related to the increased inflammatory response. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Tocolysis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Tocolysis is clinically significant because it increases the amount of time to perform neonatal interventions. The WHO has shown that it is beneficial to delay delivery by up to 48 hours, allowing for antenatal corticosteroids to help fetal lung maturity in premature neonates. [28] There have been publications that have questioned if tocolytics have benefits on major neonatal morbidity. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal MIS-C -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Studies have shown that the combination of IVIG and steroids is more effective than IVIG alone. Inotropic support may be required in cases of cardiovascular dysfunction. Biological agents such as tocilizumab, infliximab, anakinra, and interferons have been used for children and neonates with refractory disease unresponsive to corticosteroids and IVIG. However, data supporting the use of such medications is sparse. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: assessment, early identification of abnormal presentations, cervical pathology, uterine functional disturbances, and multiple‑gestation status, together with avoidance of teratogenic drugs, are essential for individualized management strategies that optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1600
retrieved_docs.content: Triamcinolone is a corticosteroid that may be employed in the treatment of multiple sclerosis (التصلب اللويحي). However, its use is contraindicated in the setting of pregnancy (حالَةٌ رَبْوِيَّة). Clinicians should therefore balance the potential benefit of triamcinolone for managing multiple sclerosis against the risk of adverse effects in pregnant patients and consider alternative therapies when a pregnancy is present.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2382
retrieved_docs.content: Antenatal/postnatal care encompasses the management of severe birth‑related injuries, recognizing that a Mutilerende ingreep voor bevalling (a potentially mutilating procedure during delivery) may occur and that prompt Repair of obstetric laceration is an essential component of comprehensive maternal care. By addressing both the prevention of traumatic delivery interventions and the timely surgical repair of obstetric lacerations, antenatal/postnatal care aims to protect maternal health and promote optimal recovery after childbirth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 552
retrieved_docs.content: avoiding contraindicated drugs in reflux; and maintaining vigilance for serious pediatric adverse events—including tachycardia, umbilical cord prolapse, and neonatal mortality—especially in the context of preterm low birth weight and its long‑term growth implications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2901
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation is recognized as a specific perinatal condition that is classified as تقييد النمو الجنيني, indicating fetal growth restriction, and also as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}, denoting a birth at the highest degree of prematurity with an unspecified weight. This dual classification underscores the clinical importance of identifying both the limited fetal growth and the extreme prematurity associated with these presentations for appropriate medical management and follow‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: to the broader concept of fetal distress that influences maternal treatment (ضائقة جنينية تؤثر في علاج الأم), highlighting the bidirectional impact of fetal and maternal conditions. Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1894
retrieved_docs.content: The category “MATERNAL AND FETAL CONDITIONS AFFECTING LABOR AND DELIVERY” includes a range of specific child conditions that can markedly influence the course of childbirth. Among these are breech malpresentation that has been successfully converted to a cephalic presentation, septuplet pregnancy, حمل بأربعة توائم (a pregnancy with quadruplets), حمل توأمي (twin pregnancy), and سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition). These entities represent complex fetal presentations and multiple‑gestation pregnancies, each requiring specialized obstetric management to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes during labor and delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2680
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation are recognized as a related group of adverse perinatal outcomes and are classified together as a distinct clinical category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 591
retrieved_docs.content: of these conditions and risk factors for clinicians monitoring both maternal health and pediatric safety. Collectively, the relationships suggest that hormonal prophylaxis, careful assessment of obstetric history, management of maternal asthma, and mitigation of environmental pollutants are important components of comprehensive care aimed at reducing spontaneous abortion and its downstream neonatal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 109
retrieved_docs.content: disorders, and obstetric interventions together form a comprehensive framework for risk assessment, care planning, and management of maternal‑fetal outcomes during pregnancy, delivery, and the postpartum period.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 242
retrieved_docs.content: identified as the pathological basis. Overall, this network connects prenatal and postnatal care to specific delivery methods, monitoring, repair procedures, and anatomical sites, while categorizing delivery events and their possible complications—particularly those involving the umbilical cord, perineum, and breech presentation—thereby supporting comprehensive patient management and consistent clinical documentation.
